figures.caption: Fig. 4: Scenarios for the Four Challenging Real-World Tasks. Each row lists the various cases for both training and testing.
Fig. 5: Examples of the Four Dexterous Manipulation Tasks. The pictures on the far left and right show the initial and final states, respectively. Each row shows the task progression over time.
TABLE I: Comparison with Baselines Across Diverse Real-World Tasks.
Fig. 2: CAD Rendering. (a) The robot dexterous hand. (b) Exploded view of the vision-enhanced fingertip structure. (c) A fabricated fingertip module.

Fig. 6: Failure Cases. (a) Case 1 shows a failure in the non-illuminatedbutton setting of the confined-box button-pressing task; (b) Case 2 includes two examples in the closed-cabinet object retrieval task: one failure with an unseen, slippery object and one success with a rough object seen during training.

Fig. 3: Whole-Body Visuomotor Policy with Fingertip Visual Perception. (a) At time t, the policy observes five fingertip RGB images (gray) and one third-view image (pink), 20 hand joint currents, and 26 arm-hand joint angles. Fingertip camera poses are computed from hand forward kinematics. Visual features are extracted using a frozen vision encoder (CLIP), while camera poses, per-finger currents, and joint angles are embedded using MLP encoders and concatenated to construct the whole-body multi-modal perception tokens. (b) Conditioned on these tokens, a transformer-based diffusion model performs K denoising steps to generate a horizon of T whole-body action tokens, which are decoded by an MLP into 26-DoF joint commands.
